product_thesis: A policy-impact simulation platform: type a proposed law and see every statute touched, every conflict created, every agency triggered, every dollar moved, every county affected, and litigation exposure BEFORE a vote. This file gathers citable evidence for why that is a large market advantage.
verification_note: Facts marked verification:'deep-fetch' were pulled from the full source page. Facts marked verification:'search-result' come from the fetched search-engine result content (title+snippet) for the cited URL and were not deep-fetched; treat their exact figures as reported-by-that-source and re-open the page before publishing if precision matters. No numbers were invented.

cost_of_bad_legislation.claim: Texas spent nearly three years and at least $768,722 of its own AG-office costs defending HB2 (abortion clinic restrictions) before the U.S. Supreme Court struck it down 5-3 as unconstitutional in Whole Woman's Health v. Hellerstedt (June 2016); a related earlier defense (Planned Parenthood v. Abbott) cost another $311,355, and the winning plaintiffs then filed to recover about $4.5 million in attorney's fees the state could be ordered to pay.
cost_of_bad_legislation.why_it_matters: The exact failure a pre-vote simulation prevents: a statute enacted, litigated for years, struck down, and then the state pays both its own defense and the other side's fees. Texas-specific, hard numbers.

cost_of_bad_legislation.claim: A Congressional Research Service review of the Unfunded Mandates Reform Act cites findings that Congress shifted at least $131 billion in costs onto states over a five-year window, and testimony in the record documents Ohio alone absorbing roughly $1.7 billion in unfunded mandates between 1992 and 1995.
cost_of_bad_legislation.why_it_matters: Quantifies the 'every dollar moved / unfunded consequence' axis: laws routinely move enormous costs onto other governments without anyone modeling it at passage.

cost_of_bad_legislation.claim: Fee-shifting after a government loss is a recurring, seven-figure line item: a Washington court ordered the state Attorney General to pay $4.3 million in attorney's fees and costs after an unsuccessful consumer-protection action against a thrift-store chain.
cost_of_bad_legislation.why_it_matters: Second concrete data point that losing on a bad legal theory is not free: courts make the losing government pay the winner's lawyers.

cost_of_bad_legislation.claim: Vulcan Technologies frames the core waste in its own YC launch: agencies 'spend millions of dollars and years hiring consultancies and law firms just to double-check that their proposed implementation of laws and executive orders is actually lawful,' and each step 'creates new arenas for political conflict, wastes taxpayer dollars,' because a change that doesn't cohere with existing state law, federal law, and case law risks litigation and loss of budget.
cost_of_bad_legislation.why_it_matters: The market leader in this exact space describes the pain in dollars-and-years terms; a simulation that surfaces the conflicts before the vote is the pre-emptive version of what they sell post-filing.

status_quo_tooling_gap.claim: Texas's status-quo bill-impact tool is the LBB fiscal note: state statute requires the Legislative Budget Board to prepare one to accompany a bill AS IT MOVES THROUGH the process (i.e., after filing/referral), the LBB has up to 15 days after receiving a bill to produce it, and by definition it is only 'a written estimate of the costs, savings, revenue gain, or revenue loss' — it does not map statutory conflicts, constitutionality, agency triggers, county-level effects, or litigation exposure.
status_quo_tooling_gap.why_it_matters: Defines the gap the product fills. The existing artifact is late (after filing), narrow (dollars only), and slow (days, human-produced). The simulation is pre-vote, multi-axis, and instant.

status_quo_tooling_gap.claim: Cost anchor for the legacy manual approach: Vulcan's Virginia engagement was a $150,000, three-month pilot (May 1 - Aug 1, 2025) that replaced a manual, line-by-line human review of roughly 24 million words of regulation. Vulcan's own CEO called Virginia's by-hand review 'the gold standard' — i.e., the alternative to the software is armies of humans reading the entire code by hand.
status_quo_tooling_gap.why_it_matters: Direct, on-point cost anchor: $150K of software stood in for a manual review of 24 million words. The '10% of legacy consultancy quotes' framing is consistent with the pilot being a five-figure line where consultancies bill six-to-seven.

status_quo_tooling_gap.claim: The upside of getting regulatory impact right is measured in billions: Virginia's regulatory-reduction effort (the process Vulcan's AI now powers) streamlined 26.8% of regulatory requirements and, per the governor's office, saves Virginia citizens over $1.2 billion per year, roughly $700 million of it from housing-code changes that shave about $24,000 off the cost of a new house.
status_quo_tooling_gap.why_it_matters: Shows the dollar magnitude on the other side of the ledger: correctly modeling what a rule/law actually does is worth billions per year in a single mid-size state.

status_quo_tooling_gap.claim: Consulting is the expensive incumbent for this work and is itself flagged as overpriced: a GSA Inspector General audit found improper pricing on a single McKinsey professional-services contract that 'may cost the United States an estimated $69 million,' and reporting on government consulting describes multimillion-dollar-per-year effective staffing costs.
status_quo_tooling_gap.why_it_matters: Establishes that the budget line the product displaces (regulatory/legal consulting) is large and already politically unpopular, strengthening the 'replace expensive consultants' wedge.

market_size.value: Government tech = 12.3% of total US tech spending of $2.9 trillion in 2026

market_size.metric: AI in government & public services market
market_size.value: $19.7B in 2025, ~19.4% CAGR 2026-2035 (GM Insights); a separate estimate puts it at $26.4B in 2025 rising to $31.1B by end of 2026 (Future Market Insights)

market_size.metric: State + local government tech spend split
market_size.value: ~$78B projected from states and ~$75B from city/county governments
$1.45B in 2024 to $3.90B by 2030 at 17.3% CAGR (Grand View); LegalTech-AI segment $2.82B in 2025 to $3.7B in 2026 at 31.4% CAGR (Research and Markets)
market_size.source: Grand View Research; Research and Markets

buyer_map.why: They draft and legally vet bills today by hand; a simulation that flags every statute touched and conflict created is a direct force-multiplier on their core job.
They already owe a fiscal note per statute; the product does the dollars-moved axis faster and adds the axes (conflicts, agencies, counties) the fiscal note omits.
They are the ones who defend enacted statutes and eat the litigation cost and fee-shifting when a law is struck; pre-vote litigation-exposure scoring is loss-avoidance they can price.

buyer_map.buyer: Government efficiency / regulatory offices (Texas TREO, Virginia ORM, DOGE-style units)
buyer_map.why: Explicitly chartered to cut red tape and cost; Virginia already institutionalized exactly this category of AI by executive order and law.
They want to model a bill's full footprint (winners, losers, counties, conflicts) before spending to push or kill it; commercial willingness-to-pay and no procurement cycle.
Fee-shifting cuts both ways: challengers want to identify the constitutionally weakest provisions to target; a conflict/precedent map is offense for them.

buyer_map.buyer: Municipal-bond insurers and rating agencies (adjacent / hypothesis)
buyer_map.why: A law that moves dollars or triggers unfunded mandates changes issuer fiscal health; impact simulation is a novel risk-underwriting input. Framed as a hypothesis, not a validated buyer.
